questions.text: Which of the following BEST describes what "trustless" means in the context of Web 3.0 and blockchain?

options.text: The blockchain network has no security - users cannot trust it with money
Users must distrust every other participant before transacting
Users do not need to trust any company or individual - they trust publicly auditable code and cryptographic proof instead
The system does not store any user data and therefore cannot be trusted to remember transactions

questions.explanation: Explanation: "Trustless" is one of the most misunderstood words in blockchain. It absolutely does NOT mean "untrustworthy" or "zero security." It means the opposite: you don't need to trust any human intermediary because the system itself - through cryptography and publicly auditable code - enforces rules automatically. When a smart contract says "if delivery confirmed, release payment," that condition will be executed identically for every user, without any human's discretion. Compare this to Swiggy's refund process, which requires trusting Swiggy's customer service team to be honest and consistent. Blockchain removes that dependency entirely.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: "Trustless" is one of the most misunderstood words in blockchain. It absolutely does NOT mean "untrustworthy" or "zero security." It means the opposite: you don't need to trust any human intermediary because the system itself - through cryptography and publicly auditable code - enforces rules automatically. When a smart contract says "if delivery confirmed, release payment," that condition will be executed identically for every user, without any human's discretion. Compare this to Swiggy's refund process, which requires trusting Swiggy's customer service team to be honest and consistent. Blockchain removes that dependency entirely.

questions.text: In the evolution of the web, which generation first introduced the concept of USER OWNERSHIP of digital assets and data?

options.text: Web 1.0 - because users could host their own websites on personal servers
Web 2.0 - because platforms gave users accounts that they "owned"
Web 3.0 - because blockchain cryptography allows true ownership of digital assets via private key control
None of the above - digital ownership is impossible without physical possession

questions.explanation: Explanation: Web 1.0 allowed anyone to host content on their own servers - a form of control, but not ownership of the open web itself. Web 2.0 gave users "accounts" but these are just username-password combinations that the platform can delete, ban, or modify at any time (YouTube channels, Instagram accounts are all platform-controlled). True ownership requires that no third party can revoke it. Only cryptographic key pairs (Web 3.0) provide this - your private key is the only way to access and control your wallet, and no company or government can change that without physically taking your device. Web 3.0 is the first time "digital ownership" has a mathematically enforceable meaning.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: Web 1.0 allowed anyone to host content on their own servers - a form of control, but not ownership of the open web itself. Web 2.0 gave users "accounts" but these are just username-password combinations that the platform can delete, ban, or modify at any time (YouTube channels, Instagram accounts are all platform-controlled). True ownership requires that no third party can revoke it. Only cryptographic key pairs (Web 3.0) provide this - your private key is the only way to access and control your wallet, and no company or government can change that without physically taking your device. Web 3.0 is the first time "digital ownership" has a mathematically enforceable meaning.

questions.text: A musician earns ₹0.007 per stream on Spotify and receives approximately 15% of that after label and distributor cuts. On a Web 3.0 platform, she earns ₹0.05 per stream with a 2% platform fee. Approximately how many times more does she earn per stream on the Web 3.0 platform?

options.text: 3× more
7× more
47× more
100× more

questions.explanation:
Calculation:
Spotify: ₹0.007 per stream × 15% to artist = ₹0.00105 per stream
Web 3.0: ₹0.05 per stream × 98% to artist = ₹0.049 per stream
Ratio = ₹0.049 ÷ ₹0.00105 = 46.67× ≈ 47×
7× is the error students make when they compare the per-stream rates (₹0.05 vs ₹0.007) without accounting for the middlemen that take the artist's share from the Spotify payout. When you correctly include the label's 85% cut from Spotify's payment, the difference becomes 47×.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C - approximately 47× more

Calculation:
Spotify: ₹0.007 per stream × 15% to artist = ₹0.00105 per stream
Web 3.0: ₹0.05 per stream × 98% to artist = ₹0.049 per stream
Ratio = ₹0.049 ÷ ₹0.00105 = 46.67× ≈ 47×
7× is the error students make when they compare the per-stream rates (₹0.05 vs ₹0.007) without accounting for the middlemen that take the artist's share from the Spotify payout. When you correctly include the label's 85% cut from Spotify's payment, the difference becomes 47×.

questions.text: What is the primary function of IPFS (InterPlanetary File System) in a Web 3.0 application?

options.text: To execute smart contracts faster than a standard blockchain
To provide a decentralised alternative to DNS (domain name system)
To store files in a decentralised manner using content-based addressing so no single company controls the data
To replace crypto wallets with simpler password-based authentication

questions.explanation: Explanation: IPFS is a peer-to-peer file storage protocol. Unlike traditional web servers (where a file lives at amazon.com/file.jpg and Amazon can delete it), IPFS stores files across thousands of nodes and addresses them by their content hash. If the content exists anywhere on the IPFS network, it's accessible - no single server to take down. This is crucial for Web 3.0: if your NFT's image is stored on IPFS, the NFT truly cannot be deleted even if the company that created it goes bankrupt. IPFS does not execute smart contracts (that's the blockchain's job), does not replace DNS (that's separate), and does not affect wallet authentication.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: IPFS is a peer-to-peer file storage protocol. Unlike traditional web servers (where a file lives at amazon.com/file.jpg and Amazon can delete it), IPFS stores files across thousands of nodes and addresses them by their content hash. If the content exists anywhere on the IPFS network, it's accessible - no single server to take down. This is crucial for Web 3.0: if your NFT's image is stored on IPFS, the NFT truly cannot be deleted even if the company that created it goes bankrupt. IPFS does not execute smart contracts (that's the blockchain's job), does not replace DNS (that's separate), and does not affect wallet authentication.

questions.text: Which of the following are genuine characteristics of Web 3.0 that distinguish it from Web 2.0? Select ALL that apply.

options.text: Users own their digital assets via cryptographic key pairs - no platform can revoke ownership
All Web 3.0 applications require users to understand cryptography in detail before using them
Smart contracts execute business logic automatically without human intermediaries
Transactions are publicly auditable and verifiable by anyone on the blockchain explorer
Web 3.0 is always faster and cheaper than Web 2.0 for all types of applications

questions.explanation: A - CORRECT: Cryptographic key pair ownership is the defining feature of Web 3.0. No platform can revoke a private key - it's a mathematical certainty.B - INCORRECT: This was true historically, but MST's SARAL Protocol (Google login → wallet creation) explicitly removes this barrier. Web 3.0 can be as easy to use as Swiggy.C - CORRECT: Smart contracts replace brokers, escrow agents, payment processors, and any human middleman. They execute identically and automatically when conditions are met.D - CORRECT: This transparency is one of Web 3.0's greatest differentiators. Any MST transaction can be verified at MSTScan.com - unlike Swiggy's opaque commission calculations.E - INCORRECT: Web 3.0 adds overhead (consensus, cryptography) that makes it slower and more expensive for simple database operations that don't need decentralisation. It's better for trust-requiring transactions, not for all applications.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answers: A, C, D

A - CORRECT: Cryptographic key pair ownership is the defining feature of Web 3.0. No platform can revoke a private key - it's a mathematical certainty.

B - INCORRECT: This was true historically, but MST's SARAL Protocol (Google login → wallet creation) explicitly removes this barrier. Web 3.0 can be as easy to use as Swiggy.

C - CORRECT: Smart contracts replace brokers, escrow agents, payment processors, and any human middleman. They execute identically and automatically when conditions are met.

D - CORRECT: This transparency is one of Web 3.0's greatest differentiators. Any MST transaction can be verified at MSTScan.com - unlike Swiggy's opaque commission calculations.

E - INCORRECT: Web 3.0 adds overhead (consensus, cryptography) that makes it slower and more expensive for simple database operations that don't need decentralisation. It's better for trust-requiring transactions, not for all applications.

questions.text: Which of the following are valid reasons why a Web 3.0 music platform pays artists MORE than Spotify, even if the fan pays the SAME monthly subscription fee? Select ALL that apply.

options.text: Smart contracts eliminate the need for record labels and distributors, removing their 55 - 70% share
The platform takes only 1 - 2% as a gas/protocol fee instead of Spotify's 30% platform fee
Web 3.0 platforms are subsidised by governments to support artists
Payments go directly to the artist's wallet via smart contract, eliminating payment processing delays and intermediary fees
Web 3.0 platforms have lower server costs because blockchain is always cheaper to operate than AWS

questions.explanation: A - CORRECT: The record label's 55%+ cut is the largest extraction in the music industry. Smart contracts can distribute royalties directly from fan payments to artist wallets, completely bypassing labels (for independent artists).B - CORRECT: Spotify's 30% platform fee is replaced by 1 - 2% gas/protocol fees on blockchain. This alone nearly doubles artist revenue, before even accounting for the label cut removal.C - INCORRECT: No such government subsidies exist. Web 3.0 platforms operate on market economics.D - CORRECT: Traditional music payments involve multiple bank transfers, label accounting cycles (monthly/quarterly), and payment processor fees. Smart contracts on MST settle instantly per stream with negligible transaction costs.E - INCORRECT: Actually, blockchain has higher computational costs than traditional databases for many operations. The benefit is not lower server costs - it's eliminated middlemen. Large Web 3.0 platforms still use off-chain servers for streaming; only payments go on-chain.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answers: A, B, D

A - CORRECT: The record label's 55%+ cut is the largest extraction in the music industry. Smart contracts can distribute royalties directly from fan payments to artist wallets, completely bypassing labels (for independent artists).

B - CORRECT: Spotify's 30% platform fee is replaced by 1 - 2% gas/protocol fees on blockchain. This alone nearly doubles artist revenue, before even accounting for the label cut removal.

C - INCORRECT: No such government subsidies exist. Web 3.0 platforms operate on market economics.

D - CORRECT: Traditional music payments involve multiple bank transfers, label accounting cycles (monthly/quarterly), and payment processor fees. Smart contracts on MST settle instantly per stream with negligible transaction costs.

E - INCORRECT: Actually, blockchain has higher computational costs than traditional databases for many operations. The benefit is not lower server costs - it's eliminated middlemen. Large Web 3.0 platforms still use off-chain servers for streaming; only payments go on-chain.

questions.text: Design a Web 3.0 alternative to Instagram. Your answer must include: (a) What problem with Instagram you are solving. (b) Who owns the content in your system and how. (c) How creators get paid (what mechanism and what cut do they receive). (d) What replaces Instagram's algorithm in your design. (e) How a user signs up without needing to understand blockchain. Name your platform.

Explain in 100 - 130 words: Why is MST Blockchain's SARAL Protocol critical for Web 3.0 mass adoption in India? Your answer must reference: (1) The specific barrier SARAL removes, (2) The technical mechanism it uses, and (3) One real Indian use case where this matters.
